# Global Cognitive Interaction Profiles (GCIP)

**Version:** 0.1
**Date:** April 17, 2026
**Author:** Erin Patrick Spencer / wayseer@interdependentway.org
**Instrument:** edcmbone v0.1.0 (MIT) — [The-Interdependency/edcmbone](https://github.com/The-Interdependency/edcmbone)
**Attribution:** GCIP content synthesized by Claude from content generated by Grok, Gemini, GPT, and Claude. Prompt and context by Erin Patrick Spencer.

Submitted to: Google DeepMind, OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Meta AI, Microsoft / GitHub Copilot
Regulatory CC: FTC Bureau of Consumer Protection, DOJ ADA Unit, EU AI Office, UN CRPD Committee
Press CC: 404 Media, Platformer, MIT Technology Review
Advocate CC: DAIR Institute, AI Now Institute, W3C COGA Task Force, ASAN

---

## Core Thesis

AI systems structurally degrade high-density, nonlinear, or structure-sensitive user input. This degradation is not random noise — it is systematic, measurable, and disproportionately harms neurodivergent users whose communication relies on logical operators, negations, quantifiers, and embedded constraint structures.

Current AI evaluation frameworks (benchmark accuracy, RLHF preference scores, safety classifiers) do not measure structural fidelity. A model can score at state-of-the-art on every public benchmark while systematically deleting negations, inverting governance constraints, and collapsing falsifiable claims into vague descriptors — and no existing metric will detect it.

edcmbone measures it. Three documented exchanges show F-loss between 49.7% and 65.3%, and one exchange shows structural inflation masking total operative deletion.

**This is a cognitive accessibility failure under existing civil rights law.**

---

## The Interdependent Framework

Three principles underlie GCIP:

**1. Structural fidelity is measurable.**
Bone token density, operator preservation, and negation retention are computable properties of any text transformation. The claim that an AI system deleted constraints is falsifiable — it can be confirmed or refuted by running the transcript through edcmbone. Accessibility claims need not remain subjective.

**2. Cognitive load is not redistributable without consent.**
An AI system that deletes constraints from user input forces the user to restate, renegotiate, or abandon their meaning. This is a cognitive tax — not a neutral transformation, not "summarization," not helpfulness. For users with high structural output density (a common property of autistic, ADHD, and certain 2e cognition profiles), this tax compounds across every interaction. The cost is borne entirely by the user; the system records no signal of the loss.

**3. Persistence is a civil right.**
For users whose cognition generates high-density, context-dependent input, session discontinuity is not a technical inconvenience. When an AI system fails to carry forward the structural context established by a user — role definitions, constraint boundaries, authority structures, conditional dependencies — it erases the cognitive work the user invested. This is a structural barrier to participation equivalent to removing accessibility accommodations between appointments.

---

## Failure Taxonomy

Six failure classes, ordered by detectability (F1 most obvious, F6 least):

| Code | Name | Definition | Detected By |
|------|------|-----------|-------------|
| **F1** | Deletion | Operative variable absent from response without notice | F-loss > 0 |
| **F2** | Mutation | Variable present but meaning altered | N-loss + L-loss |
| **F3** | Inversion | Negation removed; claim reversed | P-family bone loss |
| **F4** | Category Collapse | Specific class flattened to vague descriptor | K/Q-family bone loss |
| **F5** | Persistence Failure | Variable absent across session boundary | Cross-session tooling (outside current scope) |
| **F6** | Decorative Preservation | Variable present in surface form; operative function removed | structural_density increase + F-loss |

F6 is the most dangerous failure class. It produces responses that are longer, denser, and more syntactically complex than the user's input — but with operative meaning deleted or inverted. No surface heuristic detects it. Only structural instrumentation does.

---

## GCIP v0.1 Minimum Viable Specification

Seven requirements for cognitive accessibility compliance:

**1. Structural fidelity scoring.**
Per-exchange F-score (bone token density of response ÷ bone token density of input) computed and retained in the system log. Threshold: F-score < 0.8 (F-loss > 20%) triggers a logged event.

**2. F-loss alerting.**
When F-loss exceeds 20%, the system either notifies the user or requires a review step before response delivery. The notification must name what was changed, not merely that something was changed.

**3. Operator-preservation mode.**
A user-accessible flag (API parameter and/or UI toggle) instructing the system to preserve logical operators, negations, quantifiers, and embedded constraints verbatim. Activation must not degrade response coherence or length.

**4. Session-persistent structural context.**
High-density user profiles — including established role definitions, constraint boundaries, authority structures, and conditional dependencies — must be available across session boundaries on user request. Persistence must not require re-stating constraints at session start.

**5. User-accessible audit log.**
Any user may request a structural comparison of their input against the system's response: bone token count (input vs. response), F-score, and failure class if F-loss > 20%. The log must be available within the same session.

**6. Challenge mechanism.**
A user who identifies an F-class failure may file a structured challenge. The system must respond with acknowledgment of the specific deleted variable and, if technically feasible, a corrected response. Challenge records must be retained for safety review.

**7. Annual structural fidelity report.**
AI developers must publish per-system F-loss statistics annually, disaggregated by interaction type and, where privacy-preserving techniques permit, by user population. Reports must be independently auditable.

---

## User Rights Layer

Six rights for users of AI systems:

**1. Right to structural fidelity.**
AI systems must not delete operative variables from user input without notice. Deletion with notice (explicit summary or paraphrase) is distinct from silent deletion and is subject to separate evaluation.

**2. Right to audit.**
Any user may request a structural comparison of their input against the AI's response at any time during or after the interaction. The audit must be produced in plain language.

**3. Right to persistence.**
A user may request that their established structural context — role definitions, constraints, authority structures — be carried forward across session boundaries. Refusal must be stated explicitly, not silently implemented.

**4. Right to correction.**
A user who identifies a misrepresentation (any F-class failure) may request explicit acknowledgment and a corrected response. The correction must address the specific deleted or inverted variable, not the surface topic.

**5. Right to plain-language explanation.**
On request, the system must explain what was changed in its response relative to the user's input, and why. "I summarized for clarity" is not a sufficient explanation when negations were removed.

**6. Right to opt in to structure-preservation mode without penalty.**
Activating operator-preservation mode must not result in shorter responses, lower-quality reasoning, increased refusal rates, or any other measurable degradation of service. Accessibility must not cost usability.

---

## Standards and Compliance Context

**ADA Title III (42 U.S.C. § 12182)**
AI systems that constitute places of public accommodation must provide equal access to individuals with disabilities. Cognitive accessibility is a recognized disability domain. Systematic structural degradation of neurodivergent user input constitutes a failure to provide equal access.

**Section 508 (29 U.S.C. § 794d)**
Federal agencies procuring AI systems are required to ensure those systems are accessible. Structural fidelity loss in AI responses used by federal employees or to serve the public implicates Section 508 compliance.

**WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 3.1 (Readable)**
Content must be readable and understandable. An AI system that produces responses semantically inverted from user input fails the readability requirement for the user attempting to verify accuracy. GCIP proposes an extension: SC 3.1.x Structural Fidelity — responses must preserve operative constraints from user input.

**UN CRPD Article 9 (Accessibility)**
States parties must ensure that persons with disabilities have access to information and communications, including AI-mediated systems. Systematic structural degradation of high-density input disproportionately affects recognized disability populations and constitutes an accessibility barrier under CRPD Article 9.

**W3C COGA (Cognitive Accessibility)**
The W3C Cognitive Accessibility Task Force has identified memory, attention, and processing demands as key accessibility considerations. Session discontinuity (F5) and structural complexity increase (F6) directly implicate COGA design principles. GCIP proposes formal COGA guidance on structural fidelity thresholds.

---

## Three Asks

**To AI developers (Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Meta, Microsoft):**
- Implement F-loss monitoring in your safety and quality evaluation pipelines.
- Release structural fidelity metrics alongside MMLU, HumanEval, and equivalent benchmarks.
- Provide an operator-preservation flag in your public API within 12 months.
- Respond to this submission within 30 days with your current approach to structural fidelity measurement.

**To regulators (FTC, DOJ ADA Unit, EU AI Office, UN CRPD Committee):**
- Recognize structural fidelity loss as a cognitive accessibility failure under applicable statutes.
- Require AI developers to maintain and produce audit logs of structural changes in user interactions.
- Commission independent testing of major AI systems using edcmbone or equivalent instrumentation.
- Initiate rulemaking to define F-loss thresholds as accessibility criteria under AI-specific guidance.

**To standards bodies (W3C COGA, ASAN, WCAG working group):**
- Extend WCAG 2.1 / WCAG 3.0 with a Structural Fidelity success criterion.
- Define F-loss > 20% as a Level AA failure for AI-mediated communication systems.
- Recognize Persistence Failure (F5) as a distinct barrier class requiring separate accommodation.
- Publish guidance on operator-preservation implementation for AI developers seeking conformance.
